data.title: Central Banks Keep Gold In Focus In February
data.description:
Demand for gold from central bankers continues, with data available for February showing reported global central bank gold reserves rising by 24t. Thus far, Poland, China, Turkey and the Czech Republic have led gold demand from emerging market central banks.

Chart 1: Official gold reserves rose by a further 24t in February

Monthly reported central bank activity, tonnes*

  • National Bank of Poland (NBP) lead month and year-to-date net buying, adding 29t in February alone. Year-to-date, NBP added 32t of gold, with its total gold holdings now 480t or 20% of its total reserves
  • People’s Bank of China (PBoC) added 5t of gold in February, its fourth consecutive month of net buying since it resumed reporting in November 2024
  • The Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey added 3t of gold in February, with its gold reserves totalling 623t or 38% of its total reserves
  • The Central Bank of Jordan also added 3t of gold in February, with total gold reserves now at 72t or 30% of total reserves
  • Qatar Central Bank reported 2t of net buying in February; its total gold holdings now stand at 114t, which constitutes 19% of total reserves
  • The Czech National Bank added 2t of gold to their reserves this month, its gold holdings stand at 55t or 3% of total reserves
  • Net sellers for the month of February were the National Bank of Kazakhstan (-8t) and the Central Bank of Uzbekistan (-12t). Both central banks lead net selling activity year-to-date, down 4t
  • Despite net sales y-t-d, gold reserves form 54% of Kazakhstan's total reserves, bringing its total to 280t.

The National Bank of Belgium (NBB) has addressed recent media reports on the monetisation of Belgium’s gold reserve for public finance purposes, particularly defence spending. In a recent press release, NBB reaffirmed its independence and said gold reserves constitute assets allocated for achieving “public interest tasks entrusted to the Bank.”

Chart 2: Central banks' appetite for gold continues in February 2025

Y-t-d central bank net purchases and sales*

Original Post

data.title: Politics And The Markets 04/05/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

data.title: Multi-Asset Income In A New Regime
data.description:
By Karen Watkin, CFA & Fahd Malik

Transcript

Karen Watkin: Generating income is often in front of mind for many investors, but the market conditions today are very different from those that we’ve had over the past decade or so. So taking a flexible approach to income investing is critical.

Fahd Malik: So we focus on efficient income, and the way I would describe efficient income is focusing on income that comes from assets that have good risk-adjusted returns. And in this environment, you don’t really have to reach down in quality to generate efficient income. You can do so by buying higher-quality assets and positioning defensively. But what’s important to keep in mind in this environment is having a global multi-sector approach. You got to look for opportunities outside of the US and not just that, across sectors as well. So the places where we find attractive opportunities right now are emerging-market corporate debt, European high yield and securitized assets.

Karen Watkin: When we saw bond yields languishing towards the historical end of their ranges, it was important for investors that wanted a compelling level of income to be able to look much further afield. Your fixed-income assets can give you really attractive sources of yield, and so now it’s about being able to pivot your portfolio to think about the role that the other asset classes and strategies can bring to you. So for example, if you think about your equities, even just a few years ago, the gap between your dividend yields and high-yield credit bonds was not very much. It was less than a percent. If you fast-forward to today, actually that gap has now widened to almost 4%. So we think it makes sense to actually be far more balanced with your equities. Yes, have some of those high-dividend payers, but don’t just put all your eggs in one basket. Include some of those secular winners that can benefit from trends like AI that may not be paying much in dividends but actually give you really great upside potential.

Fahd Malik: What’s also important to remember is that policy might diverge across the globe. And what that means is you got to be nimble, not just in the overall level of duration, but in which country you take that duration in. We are matching the growth risk of a country with its duration risk. So, for instance, if you are taking credit and equity risk in the US, we are buying US duration because we think that leads to a better outcome for clients.

Karen Watkin: Taking a multi-asset approach to income investing can provide a really interesting opportunity for investors because it enables them to capture that growth alongside the income that they want to generate. It also means by having a well-diversified portfolio, that they should be able to experience a smoother path of returns, as well as getting access to a broader array of income sources.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BST: A Technology CEF Under Pressure, A Long Way To Go
data.description:
Thesis

We have not covered the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (NYSE:BST) in over two years. The name is a closed-end fund focused on technology equities, and represents a structural wrapper to extract dividends from technology stocks. In our last article, we highlighted the CEF's structural premium versus a background of valuation ratios for its underlying components.

After the significant market events from the first week of April 2025 which saw widespread selling on the back of new Trump tariffs, we are going to re-visit the fund. We will highlight for investors the best way to think about BST, and why the fund has a significant way to go until it can be considered 'cheap'. While we think the market is currently oversold, and we might see a near term bounce, longer term we are of the opinion that technology equities will be under pressure, thus translating into a weak BST forward.

A CEF is a structural wrapper. BST is a take on tech equities.

If one ignores the word CEF and just uses the word fund here, the picture becomes more clear. BST is a fund that holds tech equities:

The fund is overweight semiconductors, software and tech hardware. Its top ten names are very well known tech giants:

BST is structured as a CEF so that it can structurally provide for a high dividend yield and return of capital when needed, all within the liquidity constraints of a closed end fund. There is no 'magic' when it comes to a CEF wrapper versus a pure ETF when returns are concerned. Both structures will derive gains from the underlying equities moving up in value. Conversely, when the market sells-off, both types of instruments will face significant losses.

One aspect which is very specific to CEFs however is the premium/discount to NAV. We are going to discuss this aspect in the next section, while we will analyze the valuation background for the sector in the subsequent one.

Discount to NAV - a long way to go

While BlackRock has been very proactive in addressing chronic discounts to NAV for its CEF family via tender offers, CEFs do have a high beta to the underlying asset class:

As we can notice from the above chart, the last time tech came under pressure in 2022, the CEF saw a massive move in its premium to NAV from a +10% figure to a -5% level. This time is not going to be any different. The actions taken by BlackRock are meant to address chronic CEF issues, not wrong way market bets. If tech continues to lead on the way down, do expect BST to move to a -5% to -10% discount to NAV, consistent with its historic ranges.

Valuation concern for tech

Tech has led the way down this year, after a tremendous bull market that saw many names double in value:

As we can see from the above chart, BST has been closely tracking the Nasdaq this year, an index which is represented by the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) fund in the above chart. We have also introduced the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) in the above comparison.

Kindly note, we are using a total return metric above in order to capture the idiosyncrasies of the CEF structure, which tends to distribute gains via high-interest proceeds. The underlying risk factors are otherwise quite similar. We can observe this correlation historically as well:

While BST underperformed since the 2022 drawdown, it did much better than QQQ or XLK during the 2020/2021 bull market. The correlations are very strong, with the funds exhibiting similar compositions.

We have warned investors regarding tech and the AI theme repeatedly:

  • ROM: De-Risking Means De-Leveraging In Today's Environment: the fund is now down -20% since our 'Sell' rating
  • AIPI: Laggard In The AI Space, Unsupported Distribution: the fund is down -14% since our 'Sell' rating

The common thread in these article were the sky high starting valuations for the tech sector:

With starting P/E ratios of 28x, tech was at the beginning of the year in an astounding 87% 10-year percentile. Make no mistake about it, while it might take time, financial markets always engage in mean reversion. And usually, mean reversion also shows a propensity to overshoot to the downside.

While the last weeks have seen a decrease in P/E ratios, we are far, far away from long term fair values. Will the market just continue its way down and have a -30% drawdown this month? We think not. Even bear markets do not work that way. For any severe drawdown like the recent one, we will have some sort of re-bound once the tariff picture clears up a bit and the market is more comfortable with the overall macro. However, long term, tech will be under pressure, and given its composition BST will be too.

Distribution coverage

While the current distribution is well covered, expect changes:

Please note that the March 2025 distribution comes entirely from realized long-term gains. This purely means the CEF sold at a gain some of the holdings in its portfolio. As those gains move to losses, the fund will not have any further gains, and will thus rely on return of capital. We are not there yet, but a suddenly decreasing NAV will eventually mean zero capital gains.

Why not a Sell rating then?

The recent move has been extremely violent, with April 3, 2025 recording the largest one day move down in tech since the 2020 Covid days. This is not normal, and the markets are left severely oversold. We are due for a bounce here. The magnitude is debatable, but it will happen. Long term BST holders can make a decision after a small bounce regarding net desired position, while new entrants should think twice regarding buying into an asset class which is structurally moving lower to re-set hyperinflated valuations.

Conclusion

BST is a technology equity CEF. The name has a very high correlation to the Nasdaq, and simply represents a way to extract dividends from the tech equity asset class via the CEF wrapper. We are of the opinion that tech has a significant way down in order to re-align valuations with historic norms. Further to that BST structurally will see its discount to NAV widen, as it has done in all tech bear markets. Therefore, there is significant down pressure on the fund long term. However, on a shorter time-frame we expect a small bounce after a vicious sell-off, the largest seen since the 2020 Covid days. We are therefore a 'Hold' here, but caution new entrants to stay away, while holders might want to re-think position size after the expected bounce.

data.title: Ames National: Reducing The Earnings Estimate Following Tariff Announcements And Downgrading To Hold
data.description:
Earnings of Ames National Corporation (NASDAQ:ATLO) will likely decline this year because of tariffs and their impact on inflation and credit risk. Further, the tariffs have dampened the outlook for commercial and agricultural loan growth. On the other hand, continued margin expansion will likely lift the bottom line. The average loan yield will continue to expand as low-yielding fixed-rate loans mature. At the same time, the average deposit cost will continue to dip because of interest rate cuts. Overall, I’m expecting Ames National’s EPS to dip by 2.6% to $1.11 per share in 2025. Compared to my last report written in January 2025, I’ve reduced my EPS estimate because I’ve increased my operating expenses estimate and decreased my loan growth estimate following the most recent tariff announcements. The year-end target price is below the current market price. Based on the total expected return, I’m downgrading Ames National to a hold rating.

Loan Outlook has Sharply Deteriorated Due to Tariffs

As per the latest news reports, the U.S. Government has levied fresh tariffs on most countries of the world. However, there is a chance that the U.S. Government will roll back, negotiate down, or delay some of these new tariffs as it did last month with Canada and Mexico. There is a lot of uncertainty in that regard. However, one thing is for sure, the operating environment for most American businesses will not be conducive to investments and decision-making. Businesses need stable policies to make capital expenditure decisions. Hence, the uncertain trade policy will erode the demand for commercial loans.

The PMI indices were already showing a slowdown last month; the new tariffs should result in even more slowdown. The manufacturing PMI index worsened last month, while the services PMI index dropped close to the contractionary territory, which is below 50.

Ames National’s loan growth was already quite low last year, coming in at just 2.0% for the full year, which was below the four-year compounded annual growth of 5.1% from 2020 to 2024. Due to the outlook on commercial loans, I think 2025 will also be a year of low growth.

Apart from Commercial Loans, Ames National also focuses on residential real estate loans, which make up almost a quarter of total loans.

The company lends in the state of Iowa through its six subsidiary banks. The environment is currently not conducive to residential loan growth in that state because, firstly, mortgage rates are stubbornly high despite the 100-basis points rate cuts late last year. Secondly, Iowa’s house price index got steeper in late 2024, which is bad for housing affordability and consequently new mortgage demand.

Agriculture is another key lending area for Ames National. Unfortunately, Iowa’s agricultural segment is highly dependent on exports, which isn’t good in the ongoing trade conflict. According to the U.S. Trade Representative, Iowa is the country’s second-largest agricultural exporting state. Even more unfortunate is the fact that Iowa’s largest market is Canada, which represents 30% of the state’s total goods exports. If the Liberal Party of Canada returns to power in the elections later this month, then we can expect Canada’s commitment to retaliating to the U.S. government’s tariffs to continue.

Overall, I’m expecting the loan portfolio to grow by just 1% in 2025. Further, I’m expecting deposit growth to match loan growth. In my last report on the company, I had projected a loan growth of 4.6% for this year. My outlook has worsened considerably since the last report because of the tariffs.

Margin to Benefit from Declining Deposit Costs, Rising Loan Yields

Ames National’s net interest margin shot up by 17 basis points in the last quarter to stand at 2.38%. The growth beat my already optimistic estimate given in my last report on the company. As I had anticipated in my report, the average deposit cost declined due to the Fed funds rate cuts, while the average loan yield surged as low-yielding loans matured. Many of these maturing loans were fixed-rate, and they had been originated before the last 525 basis points up-rate cycle, so they carried very low yields. Despite the 100-basis points rate cuts of last year, the proceeds from the maturing loans were reinvested at higher yields.

For the first quarter of the year, the following two points must affect the net interest margin.

  1. The average deposit cost must stabilize as there were no Fed funds rate cuts.
  2. The average loan yield must continue to expand as fixed-rate loans mature.

As per detail given in the 10-K filing, fixed-rate and variable-rate loans maturing this year made up 21% of total loans at the end of December 2024.

For the remaining three quarters of 2025, I’m expecting the loan yields to continue to climb as low-yielding fixed-rate loans mature. I’m also expecting the average deposit cost to dip a bit because I’m still expecting a 50-basis points rate cut in 2025 despite the new tariffs that should boost inflation. I think the Fed will feel pressured to cut rates to improve the economic slowdown; therefore, I’ve decided to not change my interest rate outlook for 2025.

Overall, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by 9 basis points in 2025 from the last quarter of 2024. In my last report as well, I was expecting the margin to grow by 9 basis points from 4Q-2024. However, my average margin estimate is higher now because the fourth quarter’s margin growth exceeded my expectations. At the time of the last report, I was expecting an average net interest margin of 2.3% for 2025, whereas now I’m expecting an average margin of 2.4%.

Reducing the Earnings Estimate Because of Tariffs

Ames National’s provision expense for loan losses, non-interest income, and non-interest expenses for 2024 were all in line with my expectations given in my last report on the company. However, the actual earnings per share beat my expectations because the net interest margin was higher than I had anticipated. I had projected an EPS of $1.02 while the actual EPS reported was $1.14 for 2024.

For 2025, I’m expecting the margin growth to support earnings. Further, the positive, albeit low, loan growth will lend some support to the bottom line. However, because of the tariffs, I’m expecting inflation to drive up non-interest expenses, which will hurt earnings. I’m expecting the efficiency ratio to rise to 77.2% in 2025 from 76.6% in 2024. Compared to my last report, my outlook for non-interest expenses is worse now because of the tariffs and their impact on inflation.

Additionally, I’m assuming that the provision expense for loan losses will rise this year because there is now a greater risk of default, especially the default of loans taken by agricultural exporters. I’m expecting the provision expense to make up 0.12% of loans in 2025, up from 0.05% in 2024, but still below the 2020 level of 0.50%.

I’m also making the assumption that the non-interest income will remain stable.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report earnings of $1.11 per share for 2025, down by 2.6% year-over-year. In my last report, I projected an EPS of $1.19 per share for 2025. I’ve reduced my estimate because I’ve increased my non-interest expense estimate and reduced my loan growth estimate for the year.

Downgrading to Hold

In my last report on Ames National written in January 2025, I adopted a buy rating with a December 2025 target price of $18.7. The stock price has grown by ~0.4% since my report (including the 5% plunge on April 3 due to the tariffs), thereby outperforming the market.

I usually use the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book value (“P/TB”) multiples to value banking stocks. However, because the tariffs have affected the entire market, I think the valuation multiples assigned by the market to stocks can remain discounted to historical multiples for now. I think it’s better to use peer multiples.

ATLO MRBK HNVR CZWI CBFV Peer Average
P/E GAAP ("ttm") 14.57 9.02 11.81 10.46 11.45 10.69
P/E GAAP ("fwd") - 7.60 8.43 10.78 15.08 10.47
P/B ("ttm") 0.85 0.85 0.73 0.78 0.95 0.83
P/TB ("ttm") 0.92 0.87 0.81 0.95 1.02 0.91

Source: Seeking Alpha

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my EPS forecast gives a target price of $11.8 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my EPS forecast gives a target price of $16.6 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $14.2, which implies a 14.0% downside from the April 3 closing price of $16.56.

Ames National also offers a dividend yield of 4.8% at the current quarterly dividend rate of $0.20 per share. Adding the price downside to the dividend yield gives a total expected return of negative 9.2%. As this expected return isn’t bad enough for a sell rating, I’m going to downgrade Ames National to a hold rating.

data.title: Monthly Newsletter - March 2025
data.description:
By David Baskin

Thirteen Years and Thirteen Days

Someone (perhaps Mark Twain) said that history does not repeat itself, but it rhymes. Nowhere is this more true than in the stock market. The market drop following President Trump’s remarkable Liberation Day speech was brutal, with the major S&P 500 Index falling more than 5% in one day. However, this is hardly unique, or really, not even that unusual except for its speed. While corrections can be unnerving, they have historically been a normal part of investing. Since 1980, the S&P 500 has experienced a drop of 5% or more in 93% of calendar years and has experienced a drop of 10% or more in 47% or almost one in two calendar years.

When I sat down to write this essay, I was tempted to explain in detail why I, and every mainstream economist you can find, thinks the tariffs rolled out on April 2nd are a very bad idea. I will not do so, because if you are interested, it is very easy to find chapter and verse from many more eminent thinkers than me. Instead, in the spirit of "reuse and recycle," I have revisited an essay I wrote in 2016. Can anyone remember the market crash of 2016? I can’t either. What seemed traumatic at the moment faded into insignificance in less than a decade. (By the way, the S&P 500 returned 9.54% in 2016, not too bad!) Anyway, this is what I wrote back then.

This morning, I met with a couple who became our clients in January 2003, now thirteen years ago. The wife had called me, crying, concerned that all of their savings were going to be lost due to the ongoing market crash which she was hearing about on TV and the internet. When they arrived this morning, her hands were red from wringing and her eyes were red as well. Her husband was bemused. He didn’t know what to tell his wife.

In preparation for the meeting, I had worked up their file. Over the course of thirteen years, their portfolio had produced a compounded return of 8.7% per annum, after fees and expenses. They had a cumulative return of over $1.5 million. Even last year, with all the craziness in the markets, they made a bit of money. This year, they are down about 6% in thirteen days.

In our meeting, I pointed out that over the last thirteen years, there have been 365 thirteen-day periods. Many of them have been worse than the last thirteen days, but we cannot remember any of them. Over the course of time, they have faded into insignificance. Or perhaps more accurately, can now be recognized as insignificant, because over thirteen years, no two-week period will really matter that much.

Readers of the emerging literature in behavioral economics will recognize the cognitive errors into which our client has fallen. Recency bias, loss aversion and confirmation bias (she is a chronic pessimist) have all led to her present condition. She has focused on thirteen days and forgotten thirteen years. I also told her this: Yes. I know it’s miserable, but these are the times when we all need to listen to our heads and ignore our hearts (and guts). The flight response is very deep-seated, and we all have it hard-wired into our brains. Running away is the worst thing we could do. We saw it in 2008/09, and again during the start of the COVID crash – the only ones who ended up losing money were those who panicked. So yes, stay the course. It’s the hard thing but the right thing.

Back here in 2025, we are in terra incognita, where a giant experiment is about to take place. What happens when you apply discredited 19th century notions about trade in the 21st century? Do ideas from the age of steam when Britannia ruled the waves and America was an emerging power work in a world of robots, container ships and instantaneous communication? The markets are telling us this is a bad idea. We hope somebody is listening.

How we are navigating tariffs

Almost exactly 5 years ago, amidst the initial stages of the COVID-19 pandemic, I wrote a blog post about our framework for investing during a crisis. Today, we find ourselves in a similar situation, only this time, not from a virus, but from US President Donald Trump’s plan to implement tariffs on most countries in the world.

It is certainly understandable to be nervous about the situation, and we are as well. It is not difficult to imagine a scenario where tariffs cause both inflation and lower economic activity, leading to a dreaded state of “stagflation”. Furthermore, there could be retaliatory measures against US companies, leading to further economic slowdown around the world.

However, the situation was highly uncertain in every past stock market correction as well.

  • In 2022, inflation was running rampant, resulting from the COVID-19 stimulus and supply chain disruptions.
  • In 2020, economic activity halted nearly overnight to prevent the spread of COVID-19.
  • In 2008, the failure of Lehman Brothers led to a global credit freeze with wide potential repercussions.

In each crisis, there are certainly scenarios where things worsen and spiral out of control. However, people and governments generally try to fix things to ensure that the worst outcomes do not get realized (even if the methods are controversial), and investors who could stomach the volatility were richly rewarded when things inevitably recovered. There is never nothing to worry about, and investing is not about avoiding risk, but about deciding which risks to accept.

To be sure, there were casualties in each crisis: investors in Lehman Brothers and Washington Mutual lost all their money, while many “COVID-winners” such as Peloton never recovered. This is why we place strong emphasis on using fundamental analysis to own high-quality businesses, to have confidence that the companies we own will not only survive but gain market share in weak times.

There are two things we are doing in response to the tariff situation.

The first is to dispassionately evaluate each company’s situation with tariffs.

Apple’s stock, for example, was down by 9% on April 3. Tariffs are certainly not good for Apple, and it makes sense the stock is down. Digging deeper, however, 36% of Apple’s sales are from the United States, and of that 36%, about 27%-30% (and a higher % of profits) comes from Services such as iCloud+, AppleCare, Apple Pay, and the App Store. Almost all of Apple’s products are manufactured outside of the United States, so only about a quarter of Apple’s profitability would be directly impacted.

However, Apple has levers to offset the impact of tariffs:

  • It sells a broad range of products at different price points (iPhone 16e, iPhone 16, iPhone 16 Pro), and customers can trade down to a lower price point.
  • Most iPhones in the US are sold under installment plans, which reduces the direct impact of a price increase (a $15 monthly payment hike feels like much less than a $350 price increase), or with carrier subsidies, which pushes the tariff onto carriers like Verizon and T-Mobile.
  • Apple has some ability to shift the sourcing of US-bound products from countries with lower tariff rates such as India and Brazil and ask its manufacturers to absorb some of the impact. Furthermore, the Mac Pro is made entirely in the US already.
  • Apple can raise prices. Apple has shown it can raise prices by a bit without reducing demand before and furthermore, tariffs impact all of Apple’s competitors as well, so iPhones and Macs would not be relatively more expensive.

This is before considering the possibility that tariffs get lifted/reduced, or that Apple manages to secure exemptions, as CEO Tim Cook did during Donald Trump’s first term.

Put together, while there is likely to be some impact, I expect the ultimate impact of tariffs on Apple’s profitability to be manageable and beyond that, expect Apple to continue making innovative new products and gain market share.

The second thing we are doing is to look for ways to increase the quality of the portfolio. In periods of panic, the stock market prioritizes short-term issues (COVID lockdowns, tariffs) ahead of long-term business prospects and especially management quality. This can be a great opportunity to purchase high-quality businesses at attractive prices. Prudently managed companies can use this opportunity to make acquisitions, conduct share repurchases, and gain market share from weaker competitors, resulting in a greater bounce-back when things inevitably recover.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Arcutis Biotherapeutics: Zoryve's Growth Impresses, But Valuation Is A Concern (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Arcutis Biotherapeutics (NASDAQ:ARQT) has been on a rollercoaster since my “Sell” rating in Summer 2023.

The stock dropped nearly 80% in the months following my last look - only to rally back and then some.

The company markets Zoryve (roflumilast), a topical phosphodiesterase 4 inhibitor, for the treatment of plaque psoriasis, mild to moderate atopic dermatitis, and seborrheic dermatitis. It is also awaiting FDA approval for scalp and body psoriasis.

While I acknowledged roflumilast’s utility in plaque psoriasis, I cautioned that other investigational indications, such as seborrheic dermatitis, would “contend with intense competition.” I also expressed some concern over Arcutis’ high operating expenses.

Judging by the stock’s outperformance last year, and so far this year, Arcutis must have made notable progress. Let’s take a look!

Recent Developments

When I first visited Arcutis, Zoryve was generating no more than $5 million per quarter and Arcutis was burning (cash from operations) $60 million to $80 million every quarter. The company’s financial statements have since changed.

Revenues skyrocketed over 470% year-over-year in 2024 to $196.5 million. In Q4 2024, total revenues were $71.36 million with Zoryve generating $69.4 million of that. Cost of revenues was $6.9 million. SG&A expenses were $57.6 million. Notably, revenue growth is really outpacing SG&A expenses. The latter has, essentially, stalled.

This signals that Zoryve is generating a lot of interest on its own merit in the plaque psoriasis market. The net loss in Q4 was $7.6 million. This was much improved from a net loss of $61.2 million in Q4 2023. Impressively, Arcutis utilized only $0.7 million in operating activities. So, it is very close to generating cash.

Arcutis recognizes revenue from Zoryve product sales when its customer (pharmaceutical wholesalers and specialty pharmacy providers) obtains control (typically upon delivery). Its customers can return Zoryve. In Q4, there was a $4.1 million true-up impact to product revenue. When Zoryve was launched, the company estimated likely returns based on industry data. Because fewer products were being returned than initially planned, it had to make the $4.1 million adjustment. So, this is a positive sign as it implies that its customers are having no problem selling Zoryve.

Depending on the source, peak annual estimates for Zoryve range between $800 million and $3.8 billion. This is likely a result of the many markets Zoryve is pursuing. Psoriasis is the largest opportunity and Arcutis competes with biologics like Humira, non-injectable systemic therapies like Otezla, and topical therapies like tapinarof. Notably, Zoryve is the only topical phosphodiesterase 4 inhibitor approved for psoriasis and there doesn’t appear to be any class competition on the horizon.

The largest question mark is the IP strength for Zoryve. Because its active ingredient (roflumilast) had been previously approved (in 2011 as an oral tablet for plaque psoriasis). Zoryve was granted only a three-year exclusivity period upon its approval. This prevents the FDA from approving generic versions of the drug. However, this exclusivity period for plaque psoriasis ends in July and there are already a couple of companies “knocking at the door.” Back in March 2024, Padagis Israel Pharmaceuticals Ltd. submitted an Abbreviated New Drug Application, or ANDA, for a generic version of Zoryve cream 0.3% (plaque psoriasis). Arcutis followed with a patent infringement lawsuit, claiming that the ANDA breaches key patents that last through 2037. Earlier this week, a “pause” agreement was reached between Arcutis and Padagis that pushes back the timeline for potential generic competition for Zoryve. This, essentially, buys Arcutis more time with market exclusivity in the event Padagis’ generic is approved, pending future litigation outcomes. In Europe, Teva Pharmaceuticals (TEVA) is challenging two of Arcutis’ topical roflumilast patents. This is developing.

Financial Health

Turning to the balance sheet, as of December 31, Arcutis held $71.335 million in cash and cash equivalents. Marketable securities totaled $156.62 million. Total current assets were $335.82 million, while total current liabilities were $81.013 million. So, Arcutis has solid liquidity in the short-term. The company does owe $107.23 million in long-term debt (principal is $100 million).

Again, cash burn was negligible in Q4, so its runway should extend well into the future.

Valuation Assumptions

Arcutis’ $1.8 billion enterprise value is built on assumptions of future cash flows. I conducted a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis to uncover implicit expectations for revenue growth and profitability.

As for inputs, analyst estimates for 2025 call for $300 million in revenue. This will serve as “Year 1” in the model. 25% revenue growth serves as an average throughout the projection period. This may not seem that high at first, but achieving 25% year-over-year revenue growth for nine years is no small task. A free cash flow margin of 20% represents a realistic long-term goal for a pharmaceutical company. Everything else is pretty standard.

So, we have Arcutis eclipsing $1 billion in 2031.

Even with these optimistic assumptions, Arcutis is marginally overvalued. As I noted earlier, Zoryve’s label is expanding. In addition, the company has a number of pipeline candidates, but most are preclinical (several years out). So, for my purposes, Arcutis is Zoryve.

Next, my sensitivity analysis reveals how adjustments to revenue growth and FCF margins impact intrinsic valuation. It also reveals what the market might be pricing Arcutis’ stock for.

For simplicity, it appears the market is pricing Arcutis for greater than 25% year-over-year revenue growth and 20% FCF margins.

Lastly, my Monte Carlo simulation runs 10,000 iterations of the DCF model. Each iteration randomly varies key inputs like stock beta, growth, and margins. This gives us a more realistic and dynamic look at outcomes.

While Arcutis is a bit overvalued (based on my inputs), it is priced within reason (under the 95th percentile per share value of $20.06). Because Arcutis is priced for a lot of success already, its risk/reward profile skews towards the downside.

ARQT Stock: Investment Recommendation

In conclusion, Zoryve’s launch is going exceptionally well and the drug appears to be popular among prescribers and patients. Arcutis is nearing cash flow positivity and continuing to expand Zoryve’s label. That being said, there are some notable risks here. For starters, while patents could protect Zoryve from generic launches until 2037, it is not guaranteed. After all, roflumilast is not a novel drug and Arcutis will likely have to fight like a dog to keep Zoryve from succumbing to generics. This isn’t my main issue, though, and patent litigation outcomes are always unpredictable. My main issue is Arcutis’ valuation, which seems to be priced to perfection. So while ARQT warrants a rating upgrade to “Hold,” I’m still holding off on buying pending better entry prices. However, I can certainly see ARQT’s utility in a barbell portfolio, especially as there is still plenty of room for upside even if the company meets expectations. Finally, as Arcutis is dependent on Zoryve for most its revenues, any negative developments regarding Zoryve could cause a significant and instantaneous drop in Arcutis' valuation. So, it'd be wise to be aware of this risk.

data.title: Deere: A Question Of Fiscal Support
data.description:
Deere's (NYSE:DE) share price remains near all-time highs, despite soft fundamentals and a growing threat from tariffs. I find this somewhat surprising as I have been expecting weakness to drag Deere's stock significantly lower.

Investors are anticipating a rapid return to growth and margin improvements, but I continue to believe that this is overly optimistic. Recent poor performance has been driven by normalization after a boom rather than a cycle trough. In addition, lower farm income, elevated used inventories and tariffs all represent threats to Deere's sales and margins.

Market Conditions

Across end markets, Deere continues to face headwinds from high rates and macro uncertainty, a situation that is likely to be exacerbated by tariffs. Deere expects Ag equipment sales in North America to decline around 30% in 2025. In addition to high rates and macro uncertainty, elevated used inventory levels are also weighing on demand. In addition, farm income remains under pressure, but Deere appears to be counting on government support to largely offset this. The USDA is projecting that US crop cash receipts will be fairly flat in 2025. Income is expected to be up over 20% though due to government support. Where this situation ends up with uncertainty regarding tariffs and a government focused on reducing spending is highly uncertain, though. As a result, the extent to which the government is willing to support farm income and whether this is directed towards equipment sales will be an important determinant of Deere’s sales in coming quarters.

Small Ag and Turf sales in North America are also expected to be down around 10% in 2025. While the fundamentals of the dairy and livestock segment are strong at the moment, this isn’t leading to equipment purchases. If this holds across the entire ag segment, a rebound in farm income may not lead to improved sales.

Deere believes that fundamentals are solid within Construction and Forestry. Despite this, demand is weak, which has been attributed to interest rates, uncertainty and competition. There is also a risk that the demand environment deteriorates significantly due to a rapidly building over supply in the new housing market, which could lead to an extended period of depressed construction activity.

Construction and Forestry industry equipment sales in North America are expected to decline 5-10% in 2025. While many end markets within this segment are softening, US Government infrastructure spending remains elevated and only around 50% of ILJA funds have been committed.

Tariffs

While Deere is a major manufacturer in the US, and a net exporter of ag and turf equipment, the company will be negatively impacted by tariffs. This is due to the expected impact of tariffs on farm incomes, rising input costs and potential reciprocal tariffs.

Tariffs will increase the cost of inputs like fertilizer and could weigh on exports if other countries pursue retaliatory tariffs. Roughly 85% of the potash used in agriculture in the US is imported from Canada. Canada also supplies around 25% of the US' nitrogen fertilizer demand. China has threatened 10-15% tariffs on US agricultural goods, which is significant given that China imported close to 30 billion USD of agricultural products from the US in 2024. The trade war with China cost US farmers an estimated 27 billion USD in 2018. Farmers received around 23 billion USD in support from the federal government though.

While over 75% of the units Deere sells in the US are assembled domestically, the company has suggested that its COGS could rise by something like 5-10%. Any reciprocal tariffs could also impact Deere's sales in international markets. Large export markets for the company include Canada, Europe, Brazil and Australia. A strong dollar is also a headwind for exporters, which would impact Deere through both its own sales internationally and by reducing farm income in the US.

Financial Analysis

Deere’s revenue declined 30% YoY in Q1, with production and precision ag net sales down 37% and small ag and turf net sales down 28%. Shipping volumes were lower than expected in Q4, although Deere believes that it will recover this shortfall over the course of the year. Timing of shipments and currency translation were also headwinds during the quarter.

Deere is expecting 5.0-5.5 billion USD net income in FY25, along with 4.5-5.5 billion USD operating cash flow and around 1.6 billion USD CapEx. This outlook will be impacted by tariffs though and appears to be based on an assumption of improving demand as the year progresses. It is not really clear where this optimism comes from though given that Deere's early order program was soft and data from AEM also points to continued weakness.

In addition, Tractor Zoom data seems to suggest that used inventory remains a problem, which is not surprising given the surge in equipment sales in recent years. Deere has suggested that used inventory levels have improved in recent months. Used inventory levels remain elevated in North America though and Deere is focused on rectifying this situation.

Currency could also be a headwind in coming quarters. As a result of these headwinds, I believe that Deere's net income could come in closer to 4 billion USD in FY25.

Deere's gross margins continue to decline as its sales fall. Margins have actually held up reasonably well though, all things considered. Planned underproduction has been helping to reduce field inventory levels. This is presumably a headwind to margins at the moment though. Under production was particularly acute within Construction and Forestry. Deere's large Ag field inventory was down 25% YoY at the end of 2024 and now sits below pre-COVID levels. Reduced inventory is supportive of pricing though, despite the soft demand environment. I question how long Deere can continue pushing higher pricing in an environment of declining sales and depressed farm incomes.

Deere's net income was down 50% YoY in the first quarter to 869 million USD. While it will take some time for tariffs to flow through Deere's COGS, I can see tariffs creating something like a 4% hit to its margins, which, I believe, will make it difficult for the company to hit its full-year profitability target.

Financial Services net income totalled 230 million USD in Q1, although was boosted by the sale of Banco John Deere. Underlying Financial Services net income declined due to higher provisions for credit losses. Deere expects 750 million UDS Financial Services net income in FY25, with lower provision for credit losses somewhat offset by less favorable financing spreads. I question whether the expectation of lower credit losses is realistic in the current environment, though.

Deere's job openings are certainly supportive of a rapid rebound in production and sales. While I believe a meaningful improvement from Q1 is likely, I still think the current macro environment will make it difficult for Deere to meet current expectations.

Conclusion

Given that farm income is likely to remain under pressure in the near term, a rebound in Deere's sales is largely dependent on government support for farmers. While some level of support is likely, it will probably be insufficient to fully offset lower commodity prices and tariff headwinds. Even if farm income were to rise, I question whether this would lead to increased equipment sales, as illustrated by current weak dairy and livestock equipment sales, despite strong fundamentals in the segment.

Analysts are projecting a return to YoY growth in the fourth quarter of FY25. Revenue and margins are expected to continue improving from there, although aren't expected to return to the highs of 2023 anytime soon.

The forward path of Deere's share price is dependent on whether current sales represent a cycle trough or a normalization after an unsustainable post-COVID surge. I continue to believe that Deere's sales and margins are normalizing and that the company's revenue multiple will return towards pre-COVID levels as this situation becomes accepted. Even a multiple of 3.5x FY26 sales would see Deere's share price decline to around 300 USD per share.

data.title: ETHE: Is Ethereum Sending A Signal?
data.description:
April 4th brings another session of turmoil and panic in global markets. Equities, commodities, and precious metals are all experiencing another day of deep, across-the-board losses. Of all markets, it is cryptocurrencies that are actually holding up well, with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Ethereum (ETH-USD) both green during April 4th trading as of article submission. Frankly, this is a somewhat perplexing development, but I'll get into why I believe there is perhaps a signal in the apparent 'crypto immunity' from global tariff concerns. In this Grayscale Ethereum Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:ETHE) article, we'll get into ETH's correlation with tech stocks, holder data, and recent capital flows.

Digital Asset Tariff Immunity?

Let's start with the obvious; cryptocurrencies and digital assets don't actually exist. Shocker, I know. But it happens to be true. Thus, in a world with trade-war escalation, assets that are not bound by physics or geographic borders have perhaps understandably shown little regard for tariff concerns since 'Liberation Day.'

The chart above shows BTC and ETH against proxies for the Nasdaq and S&P 500 as well as Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) and Crude Oil (CL1:COM). Through 2pm on April 4th BTC and ETH are holding up while virtually every other major asset or index is down by at least 3%. Digital assets theoretically being immune from jurisdiction risk regarding tariffs is really only one part of this, however. What I still find very perplexing about the action for BTC and ETH is that during stock market panics like the one we're seeing in real time, we often see everything down together as market participants raise liquidity any way they can.

For instance, it's not out of the realm of possibility that profitable Gold positions could be sold by speculators who are de-risking while equities fall apart. One would think BTC and ETH would be getting dragged down in sympathy with the rest of the market due to possible margin calls. But that is not happening as the week of March 31st draws to a close.

It's hard to truly express how bizarre this is because these assets are typically tightly correlated with US equities, particularly tech stocks like the Magnificent Seven. Between mid-March and today, only Tesla (TSLA) has seen correlation with ETH dip below 65%. Most of the stocks shown in the chart above had correlations with ETH in excess of 80%.

Could it truly be that tariffs don't impact cryptos? I'm actually much less sold on that possibility; especially for Bitcoin, which has a US mining footprint that requires machines produced overseas for hashing. This is not a concern for ETH, since Ethereum is a Proof-of-Stake network. Perhaps more likely? Down nearly 60% since mid-December, ETH has already sold off so bad that it's possible that there is simply nobody left who is willing to sell - even raising liquidity could alleviate problems elsewhere.

At 53% of holders currently 'out of the money,' this level of token holders hanging onto losing positions hasn't been seen in ETH in about 5 years. So what do we do with this information? I've been more cautious about Ethereum in recent months on what I view to be a weakening fundamental story, given the network's lack of fees and scaling through secondary layers that don't require nearly as much ETH for gas payment. Given that, it's hard for me to say 'buy ETH' based on any fundamental improvement in the market. But there are other considerations to keep in mind.

Seasonality and Capital Flows

I think we have to take some of the seasonality information with a grain of salt because it flat out did not work in January, February, or March. That said, April and May have historically been the best two months of the year for ETH based on mean change. That figure is 23% for April (2025 is included here) and 31% for May. For me, the bigger thing to pay attention to is whether capital is flowing in or out of ETH-based financial products.

Asset (mil) MTD Flows YTD Flows AUM
Bitcoin -$826 $1,519 $114,475
Ethereum -$370 $316 $9,565
Multi-asset -$206 -$137 $6,366
Solana $27 $86 $1,194
XRP $15 $168 $957

Source: CoinShares, as of March 28th

We know that capital exited ETH products during the last full month of March. However, year to date flow for ETH was still positive at $316 million at the end of the month.

Even though we've seen a little under $60 million come out of Ethereum ETFs so far through three full days in April, the majority of that outflow is from a $31 million single day decline in ETHE on April 2nd. On that same day, an additional $20.2 million came out of the iShares Ethereum ETF (ETHA). The problem ultimately is that ETHE's outflow on the 2nd isn't indicative of Grayscale 'fee flight' continuation:

In fact, ETH ETF share of on-chain supply still favors ETHE which commands over a third of the total market at 38.3%. Importantly, ETHE holding firm in supply share for most of 2025 is probably more indicative of a lack of demand for other funds. For instance, it took a little over 4 months for the iShares product to become the largest ETF holder of Bitcoin in the US spot ETF market. We're 8 months into ETH ETFs in the US market, and ETHE still has a 43% supply edge over ETHA. I read this primarily as a lack of investment demand for Ethereum more broadly.

Closing Takeaways

To answer the question from the headline from the title, I do think ETH is sending a signal. On a day filled with motivated sellers, ETH holders were not among them. Ultimately though, I see more than just one signal in the data. While it's quite encouraging that ETH didn't fall apart with the rest of the market on April 4th, it's perhaps more of an indication that ETH isn't actually as far along with traditional investors as digital asset holders might have hoped. Perhaps the reason ETH isn't getting slammed with the rest of the market is because there just aren't enough people holding it to begin with?

Bottom pickers may find the $1,800 per coin level to be an interesting enough area to make a contrarian trade attempt. I'll re-share something I said in March about how I still currently view ETH technicals:

I see $2,100 per coin serving as support on weekly closes going back to late 2023. That level was breached on a close the week of March 3rd, failed a back test the week of March 10th, and is currently still under that key level as of article submission on March 20th. I, personally, would not entertain taking a stab at that trade from the long side until we see the coin take back $2,100 on a weekly close.

The lack of demand for products like ETHA and similarly lower-priced spot ETFs might remain a headwind of Ethereum bulls should holders keep selling out of ETHE positions. In spite of real problems with ETH's network activity, you could still talk me into holding spot ETH ETFs, but ETHE isn't one of them.

data.title: WTI Oil Technical Outlook: Bears Are Gaining Strength For A Major Bearish Breakdown
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Since our last publication, the price actions of West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) have staged the expected corrective rebound sequence and rallied by 7.2% to print an intraday high of US$72.48/barrel on 2 April (a whisker below the US$73.50 medium-term resistance highlighted in our report) before the announcement of “US Liberation Day” reciprocal trade tariffs.

The West Texas Oil CFD has shaped a significant bearish reversal ex-post “US Liberation Day”, and plummeted by 6% on 3 April, its worst daily loss since early September 2022.

Headwinds from weak demand and excess supply

Fig 1: EIA US crude oil inventories excluding SPR (y/y change) with WTI crude oil futures as of 28 Mar 2025 (Source: MacroMicro)

WTI crude has a negative double whammy hit caused by demand and supply factors. Firstly, the rising probability of a stagflation environment in the US has triggered a global recession alarm, in turn, weakening demand for oil as business activities slow down.

Secondly, OPEC+ producers have agreed to boost the cartel's oil output by 411,000 barrels per day next month, coupled with a recent lesser magnitude of drawdown of US crude oil inventories seen in the past three weeks that may add another layer of downside pressure in oil prices.

The growth of US crude oil inventories excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) on a year-on-year basis has an indirect correlation with the movement of WTI crude oil, as build-up in oil inventories puts downside pressure on oil prices.

Since 13 December 2024, the drawn down of US crude oil inventories (excluding SPR) has slowed down from -5.11% y/y to -2.58% y/y as of 28 March 2025 based on data from the US Energy Information Administration (EIA) (see Fig 1).

US65.40 key support is looking vulnerable

Fig 2: West Texas Oil CFD medium-term trend as of 4 Apr 2025 (Source: TradingView)

Current intraday price actions of the West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) have recorded a further drop of 3.2% on Friday, 4 April, and breached below the major “Descending Triangle” range support of US$65.40 at this time of writing (see Fig 2).

In addition, the daily MACD trend indicator has just flashed out a bearish crossover condition, and inched below its centreline, which increases the probability of a major bearish breakdown below the US$65.40 major range support.

A daily close below US$65.40 may trigger the start of a medium-term bearish impulsive down move sequence to expose the next medium-term support zone of US$$60.20/58.80.

However, a clearance above the US$72.50 medium-term pivotal resistance (also the key 200-day moving average) invalidates the bearish scenario to kickstart another round of choppy corrective rebound phase for the next medium-term resistances to come in at US$76.00 and US$80.30

Original Post
